locked_facts: Heat conduction rate: Q/t = KA(T₁ - T₂)/L
Thermal resistance: R_th = L/(KA) — analogous to electrical resistance
Series combination: R_total = R₁ + R₂ (slabs in series, same heat flow)
Temperature distribution in a uniform rod is linear in steady state
Metals have high K (good conductors); wood, air have low K (good insulators)
Effective K for composite wall: K_eff = L_total/(Σ Lᵢ/Kᵢ) for series
truth_statements: Thermal resistance is analogous to electrical resistance and obeys the same series and parallel combination rules
In steady state the rate of heat flow through each layer of a composite wall is the same
The temperature drops across each layer are proportional to the thermal resistance of that layer
A thin layer of air is an excellent insulator because air has very low thermal conductivity
Double-walled flasks use the low conductivity of vacuum and air to minimise heat transfer

aha_moment.what_to_show: A composite wall of two materials (brick + insulation) in series. The temperature drop across each layer is proportional to its thermal resistance R = L/(KA). The insulation layer (low K, high R) has a MUCH larger temperature drop despite being thinner. The electric circuit analogy with resistors in series makes the calculation intuitive.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Temperature profile through composite wall showing large drop across insulator, small drop across conductor — with circuit analogy alongside
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students expect equal temperature drop across equal thicknesses — seeing the resistance-weighted distribution connected to the electrical analogy makes composite walls simple

simulation_states.label: Single slab conduction
simulation_states.description: A uniform slab with hot side T₁ and cold side T₂. Q/t = KA(T₁-T₂)/L. Temperature varies linearly through the slab. Higher K = more heat flow.
simulation_states.emphasis: Heat flows from hot to cold at a rate proportional to K, A, and ΔT, inversely to L

simulation_states.label: Thermal resistance concept
simulation_states.description: R_th = L/(KA) — thicker or less conductive means higher resistance. Compare: metal rod (low R) vs wood rod (high R) of same size. Circuit analogy: Q/t = ΔT/R_th (like I = V/R).
simulation_states.emphasis: Thermal resistance is the direct analogue of electrical resistance

simulation_states.label: Composite wall (series)
simulation_states.description: Two slabs in series (brick + insulation). Same heat flows through both. R_total = R₁ + R₂. Temperature at junction found from ΔT₁/ΔT₂ = R₁/R₂. Insulation layer has larger temperature drop.
simulation_states.emphasis: Series: same heat flow, temperatures drop in proportion to resistance

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student builds composite walls from different materials. Temperature profile shown. Add/remove insulation layers. Compare series vs parallel arrangements. Heat flow rate calculated.
simulation_states.emphasis: Add insulation — watch heat flow drop dramatically. Compare different material combinations.

common_misconceptions.belief: Thicker walls always insulate better regardless of material
common_misconceptions.correction: Insulation depends on thermal resistance R = L/(KA). A thin layer of good insulator (low K) can have higher R than a thick layer of conductor (high K). Material matters as much as thickness.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show thin insulation layer outperforming thick brick wall in reducing heat flow

common_misconceptions.belief: Temperature drops equally across equal thicknesses
common_misconceptions.correction: Temperature drop across a layer is proportional to its thermal resistance: ΔT = (Q/t) × R. A layer with higher R (lower K or greater L) has a larger temperature drop, even if thinner than its neighbour.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Composite wall with unequal temperature drops across layers of different K
